items.q: Did persecution destroy the early Church?
items.correct: No - Acts 8 records that scattered believers preached wherever they went, so persecution actually helped spread the message beyond Jerusalem.
items.misconception: Persecution nearly wiped out Christianity in its first years.
items.random_wrong: The early Church was never persecuted by anyone.
items.why: The surprising growth-under-pressure pattern is a key theme of Acts that students often invert.

items.q: Were the first Christians a completely separate religion from Judaism at the start?
items.correct: No - the first believers were Jews who worshipped in the Temple and synagogues; separation from Judaism happened gradually as Gentiles joined.
items.misconception: Christianity began as an entirely separate religion on the day of Pentecost.
items.random_wrong: The first Christians were Roman officials.
items.why: The Jewish roots of the Church explain the circumcision controversy and the Council of Jerusalem, which make no sense without them.

items.q: Was Paul one of the twelve apostles who followed Jesus in Galilee?
items.correct: No - Paul never followed the earthly Jesus; he claimed apostleship through his encounter with the risen Christ, while Matthias had replaced Judas among the Twelve.
items.misconception: Paul was one of the original twelve disciples.
items.random_wrong: Paul was the brother of Jesus.
items.why: Paul's fame leads students to slot him into the Twelve; his outsider status is what makes his conversion so significant.

items.q: On which festival day did the Holy Spirit come upon the disciples in Acts 2?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Pentecost. “Passover” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose vision led to the baptism of the Gentile centurion Cornelius?
items.why: It's Peter's. “Paul's” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who became known as the apostle to the Gentiles after his conversion on the Damascus road?
items.why: It's Paul. “Peter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How does Acts describe the life of the first believers?
items.correct: They shared possessions and met for teaching, breaking of bread and prayer
items.misconception: They lived as hermits in the desert
items.why: Correct answer: They shared possessions and met for teaching, breaking of bread and prayer. “They lived as hermits in the desert” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many missionary journeys of Paul does the Book of Acts describe?
items.why: The real figure is 3 journeys. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
